I have had the Patricia II wedge in black for about 1 year & wore them regularly in season. When I saw the Patricia at a good price in navy (from 6pm), I purchased them because I thought they would fit just like my Patricia IIs. I was wrong, and paid the price with return shipping that 6pm doesn't pay.

The crocs website says that crocs aren't suppose to fit like other sandals - they are suppose to be looser & thus more comfortable - I normally wear an 8-1/2, so have now tried both an 8 & a 9 in the Patricia shoe (I have an 8 in the Patricia II). The Patricia 9 swims on my feet & they would be a hazard to walk around in. The size 8 fits my left foot (which is my wider foot) but is too narrow on my right foot. When I placed the shoes sole to sole, I did notice a slight difference in the width, which, apparently, my foot notices too. I can only conclude a manufacturing defect. But, it is this shoe specifically or the form for this shoe? (others have written the shoe is narrow).

Consequently, if you have a wider foot, order the Patricia II instead of this one & if you are a 1/2 size, order down, not up.

My mom always had these shoes for my sister and I when we were babies (or a very similar style), so I thought they'd be great everyday shoes for my one year old.  She already had shoes she was about to grow out of, so I got the next size up in advance so we'd have them when she was ready to wear them.  I tried them on her when they arrived to check on the width issue based on other reviews, and it seemed she'd be able to wear them.  In the six weeks since then, her feet have not just grown in length but width as well, because now that her feet are long enough for the shoes, there is absolutely no way I can velcro these shut without squeezing her poor little feet!  In addition to that, the first time I even tried to get them on her, the loop that holds the strap in the middle popped right open (the seam came apart).  I immediately got on my computer to print a return receipt only to discover the return policy is only 30 days on these (I'll admit I'm used to returning items to stores that have 90 day policies).  Now I have a pair of damaged, too-small shoes that we will ever even use.  What a waste!!

I don't understand all of the good reviews these hiking shoes are getting, I bought a pair about 14 months ago, never used them on rough terrain, mostly trails and sidewalks, and I walk between 1 and 3 miles a day. After owning the boots a couple months a piece of rubber broke off the front of the sole, but it was no big deal, it was by the toe and not the part that touches the ground.

They are advertised to be water proof, but one day about a week after buying them I was out walking in a very heavy rain, and walked through a few shallow puddles a couple inches deep, by the time I got home my feet were soaked, so much for them being water proof, actually I would not wear them if it was raining hard or I expected to be walking in wet grass or whatever because the water always soaked through.

The final straw was about 4 months ago when the soles separated from the uppers on both shoes, the holes were big enough for me to put three fingers in by the time I stopped wearing the boots.

Possibly the worst shoe I have ever bought. That is not a hyperbole. Take a look at the photos. In less than 5 miles, I repeat, less than 5 miles, these shoes were destroyed. Literally falling apart. This was not even a trail run. This was a hike on a very laid back trail. The lugs were tearing and ripping off. Take a look at the pics. One of the red lugs actually ripped off. 1/4 of all the lugs had torn, in less than 5 miles.

Alright, so there is zero durability in these shoes. I mean, if you can't even get a sole right, I don't expect you to get the rest of the shoe right, and they didn't. The shoe narrows and is not as wide as I had hoped for. I had to wear a size .5 larger than normal, to allow my small toes room. This is because the shoe narrows to a point, as opposed to slowly tapering towards the big toes. Merrell has this down, Vivo does not. The shoes were slopply, lacked grip and durability. I mean, I just can't say enough bad things about this shoes. The lacing system is just dumb. Just put normal laces on your shoe. They don't stay tight, and they don't allow you to customize the fit, as much as normal laces.

Trail Condition: Rocky
User weight: 175

CONS:
-Zero durability - lugs tore off and separated in less than 5 miles of walking - side of shoe tore
-Fit is all wrong - front of shoe comes to a sharp point which means you have to size up to fit your small toes in. Shoe is narrow in comparison to many other barefoot brands.
-Lack of grip - you would think it would be superb and that is why the sole is so mushy, but no, the sole is mushy and not grippy
-Ugly as can be
-Too much cush, and still does not protect from rocks

I'm done, I'm trying to think of the best way to describe how awful this shoe is. Just don't buy it. Honestly, I'll never buy another vivo and will steer others away.

UPDATE:  I ended up returning these sandals because the clasp mechanism is simply too difficult to use.... Left me feeling frustrated every time I tried. I cannot bend around my leg and see the ankle well enough to use  a .25 inch  hook/ clasp. Too bad because they are cute. ********************************************************************* These are attractive comfortable shoes. Unfortunately the clasp/ buckle system is not explained anywhere... and as a result the last reviewer returned the sandals. Manufacturer take notice: INCLUDE INSTRUCTIONS!!  It is a sort of quick release system that I figured out after 10 minutes of playing with it... Almost gave up. You never actually "unbuckle" the strap you just slide the buckle part upwards to remove it from a small U shaped metal piece located on the the rear strap. To re- attach you slide the front strap with buckle back into the u shaped  piece attached to the rear strap. There is a small amount of elastic on the U shaped section to help realign the straps together. In my opinion this elastic could be a lot longer.... and easier to see, easier to attach, easier to notice something is going on here..  It may take some getting used to this system because it is hard to bend my head far enough over the side of my legs to see over the ankle. You have to be a contortionist :-)  Otherwise these are nice looking, feel good on the feet, no rubbing, smooth foot bed with a bit of cushioning. Could have more arch support. And the 2nd strap on the heel of the sandal could use a bit of elastic to make it more of a custom fit.  True to size. I generally take an 8- 8.5 . Need the 8.5 for moderately wide feet (not enough to warrant buying a wide width tho)  The 8 medium fit perfectly

I would actually recommend people buying this product; so why the two stars? Having had "stripper" heals in the past, that I paid a fortune for, I know how the platform should feel. This shoe is definitely a lot lighter than the higher quality products. It does feel cheap, but that's reflecting in its price and something I was actually expecting. Upon opening the package (discrete and arrived early) there was a white powdered substance in the package wrap and shoebox. I was a bit concerned at first, but started thinking it was bc of the fabric used on the shoe-- uh, yeah, no. These shoes are definitely not white, but more of an off white-grey tint color. It looked dirty. The shoebox was beat up and raggedy (it's a good thing I didn't buy it for the box), but could've been from shipping? Reading past reviews, I decided to order a size up. I'm definitely not sure what all the commotion was about (size being too small and the strap being too tight- it must've been their first pair of platforms or they have fat wide feet) because mine were a tad bit too big and I feel I would've been ok with my original size 9. FYI ladies, the strap is supposed to be snug, which holds your foot in place. If you are buying these shoes for pole dancing class, to work the stage or anything else that would require a significant amount of movement, you might want to invest in a higher quality product, but if you are buying this shoe to look sexy in lingerie for your significant other- this will definitely work and do its job. This product is definitely cheaply made and is not capable of going the distance, which brings me to my final remark-- the tread to the shoe was already falling off as I pulled the shoe out of the box. I've attached pictures just in case. I would still recommend this product (I really am not going to cry over the shoe being grey or how the tread was coming off- nothing glue couldn't fix); it's not like I'm going to be wearing these outside the house for the world to see. It does its job magnificently and I would consider to buy another pair for the price. *Note: I still have no clue what the white powdered substance was.d to see. After reading mixed reviews here about the sizing, I went with my regular size (8), figuring a little snug would be a good thing--no flopping around. While these are just (barely) wide enough, they are not long enough from heel to "annoying toe thong". That toe thong just pulls backwards into the "toe crotch" and is irritating at best. I want sandals I can forget about.
Here's the frustrating part: While Amazon provides free shipping and free returns in size 8 (I am a Prime Member) the size 9 is $5.00 more, will cost $13.98 to ship, and no free returns. What gives?? I will be returning these, but will not be trying the 9, it's just not worth the cost or the risk that they won't work either.
And I really WANT to try the 9, because here's what I do like about them: The footbed is very comfortable. The toe thong is smooth and seems like it would be very comfortable if it wasn't so tight. They are almost flat (very little drop) which is what I am looking for, and the fairly thin soles are quite flexible (most flat sandals have a stiff sole that doesn't allow my foot to articulate properly). There is not much in the way of arch support, but I don't need that. Finally, I think they are really cute, and surprisingly sexy. Sigh. The search for the perfect flat sandal continues...

I started wearing Crocs when I began college in Boulder, CO, which is the birthplace of Crocs. I'll continue to wear Crocs out of loyalty, but I'm not ecstatic about these "classic" Crocs. I put classic in quotation marks, because Crocs loyalists know that they were once made in the USA, and the shoes that were made in the USA were sized in ranges and were made of superior material that was a tad bit softer. Please know that I'm traditionally not a hooah-hooah "Made in the USA" minion, but there are distinct differences between the USA- and Mexican-made Crocs. Crocs made in Mexico are made of a similar (but different) material that has a slippery finish, and they are sized in whole-sizes only. I resisted buying Mexican Crocs because of these material and fit factors, and so I wore down my USA Crocs to the end.

I wear a 10.5 running show, and in the USA size Crocs I wore men's XL (10-11), which were perfect! For the Mexican Crocs I ordered M11, but they were too large: too much play in the toes and too much length between the heel of the shoe and the heel of the foot. I returned them for Mexican M10, which is what I settled on. They're good with room in the toe, but I would have liked an extra half-inch on the heel. This is why the USA sizing scheme was better FOR ME. I acknowledge all feet are different.

Some will say that the changes in workmanship came after the supposed slit of the company's founders due to a nasty divorce. Search for that on the internet and decide for yourself.

Background: My work involves walking long distances, in all sorts of terrain, vegetation and weather,  200+ days a year. I have been recommending Rockies for years as a quality boot at a decent price, and could count on them being comfy with no break-in, straight from the box. Not this pair - they didn't feel quite right just sitting at my desk, and I was unable to walk 30 feet across the office without extreme pain. It felt like a marble had been sewn into the seam where the upper (vertical) part of the boot meets the top of the foot, near the laces on the medial/big toe side of both boots. There was a lump beneath the liner - not sure if it was misaligned leather or bunched up insulation or what. The design has changed a little since my last pair of Cornstalkers and I think my very first pair had real Vibram lug soles, which have better traction. All in all, pretty disappointed, hopefully this pair of boots was a fluke and not a sign of a decline in Rocky quality. I think I'll go back to brick and mortar shopping so I can inspect my purchase first.

My perspective: I am a long distance runner. I run from 4-25 miles so the amount of miles daily can vary. I have had some challenges with injuries over the last year as well starting with metatarsal bone bruising and ended up now with a MCL Sprain and Pes Anserinus tendinitis. I spend a lot of time looking for the right shoes. I have both the NB Vazee Pace and the Vazee Rush. Of the two I prefer the Rush (though many of my runner friends prefer to Pace).

This shoe is built on the same last as the New Balance Zante which means it has a fairly snug midfoot and a decent size toe box. It has more toe box height for me then the Zante and more than the Pace. The overlays are structured in such a way as they don't hit any of my hot spots. The cushioning is New Balance RevLite (I think) and this is a much firmer material that has good energy return and spring. However it is NOT mushy/cushy or like walking on clouds. The heel/toe drop is 6mm which is middle of the line. The toe is upturned which does give you a feeling of springing forward. I find this shoe very comfortable to run in and can do long distances. It doesn't have a ton of support - however it's not a minimal shoe. The heel, in particular, is not super structured nor does it have hard plastic in it - this is music to my achilles which often react to other shoes with very plastic/hard heel counters. These do stretch a bit but like any running shoe you should not need to break them in. The vast huge majority of people I see buying running shoes are buying them WAY TOO SMALL. I cannot tell you how many people I see complain and I can SEE the tip of their toe is practically coming out the front. You should have at least one fingers width from the top of your longest toe to the tip of the shoe - and if you go long distances you can often desire even a bit more. I think it's vanity that people insist they wear a X size shoe. I've seen feet so stuffed into shoes it looks like they are have rolled sausages and one hit will cause them all the flesh to pop out like those old biscuit packages! Stop buying shoes too small folks. Who cares what the size is. My size in these is 1 full size larger then many of my street shoes but about normal for me in running shoes. I wear a 6.5M or 6.5W and sometimes a 7M normally in flat's or heels. I bought these in 7.5M and they are just fine. If you have a foot that usually fits a M then just make sure you are sized appropriately. If your foot tends to run wider (but not so wide you always buy wide shoes) then make sure you go up at least a 1/2 size...or buy the wide 1/2 size longer. These are consistent with my other shoes for length but they are a bit pointy so take that into consideration.

If you are someone who wants cushy marshmallow cushion then you should look more at the New Balance Fresh Foam line (as revised) because they are softer and more "cushy" and also wonderful shoes for all distance running. I've been wearing my Rush all day on weekends and walking all over and doing some of my running (I rotate shoes to prevent injuries) and they have satisfied me in all conditions. I highly recommend them IF you are someone who likes a firmer shoe but with good rebound and energy, likes mid range drops (4-8MM), does not require a ton of stability or support, and just really likes a comfortable non confining shoe that is great for multi purpose!one hit will cause them all the flesh to pop out like those old biscuit packages! My #1 complaint is the sandal will not stay under my foot.  They allow my heel to move off the sandal to the outside after only six or seven steps.  Several attempts to adjust the strap has not helped this situation at all.  #2, I walk "out" of my sandals several times a day.  You cannot relax and forget about them, as you must continually concentrate on keeping them on your feet, otherwise you'll find yourself shoe-less.  This sandal would be a waste of your money even if you only paid $3.00.  The other Adidas sandal which looks very similar to this one is an excellent choice.  This one is not!

I purchased the Black Micorsuede shoe, and went down a size according to Amazon specifications. While this shoe DOES fit, due to the fact that I wanted to insert some of those squishy shoe pads, I returned them for a size up. They have a hard sole, but the shoes themselves are exceptionally flexible and appear to be made out of a kind of suede-like material that easily absorbs water. They have a velvety type texture to them, in case you were wondering how suede-y they are. I gave them 3/5 stars because the shoes are cheap and good for a costume I want to wear them with, I just don't think they'll hold up very well beyond a weekend's wear.

The shoes I got actually didn’t have any kind or odor at all, unless you put your nose within an inch of the shoes, and at that close there was no smell of gasoline. They actually smell sort of like new shoes.

The size 6 I got (I wear a 6.5 or 7) was about 9 5/8” long, from heel to toe and 3 ½” wide from the widest part where the toes are.

PROS:
-Cheap and lightweight.
-Fast shipping.
-Fits fairly true to size. (I wear 6.5 and the size 6 would have been fine if I didn’t want to put shoe inserts in them.)
-True to color.
-The suede felt very soft, and wasn’t worn away.
-Little to no odor (like many people complained).
-The flexible nature of the shoes allow you to stretch out your toes.

CONS:
-Cheaply constructed.
-Very squishy and flexible canvas-like material, so I can see these wearing out quickly.
-Definitely not the kind of shoes you’ll want to wear in the rain, or even after a rain. They will soak up water quickly.
-While these shoes have a loop to pull through the buckle, there aren't any holes to sufficiently tighten the shoes. If you want holes, you’ll have to make them yourself.

TIPS:
If you’re going to wear shoe inserts (which I would recommend since these shoes are very cheap), you should go true to size. For example, I’m a 6.5 or 7, and the size 6 was too small to allow inserts, despite the website saying the black shoes should go down a size for being large.r example, I’m a 6.5 or 7, and the size 6 was too small to allow inserts, despite the website saying the black shoes should go down a size for being large.

I just bought these locally and I can tell you they look even better in person than the photos. I am neurotic about my shoes fitting and am consistently disappointed in fit within 6 months after almost any shoe or boot purchase. On the metal sizing tool at most shoe stores, I am an 9D; in most sneakers, I am an 8.5D; in work, hiking and cowboy boots, I vary from 8.0E-8.5D. Bottom line, my foot size is pretty random. Couple this with variances among brands and models, I feel like a hopeless case. Physical context: I'm 5'7 and range from 175 to 200 lbs.  On my previous boot purchase, I ordered 4 pairs of the same boot in order to get the right fit. Sure enough, 6 months later, I was disappointed. The boots had broken in so much that my feet were swimming in them.  So today, when I bought the Lucchesies, the sales gal convince me to ditch my socks and put on these pantyhose footy things.  With them, I got my feet pretty snug into an 8D (as mentioned, I often end up with an 8-1/2 or an 8E).  I'm home now, and I've been wearing them with the aforementioned fru-fru socks for an hour and my feet are really settling down. I have high hopes in these stretching out to be the best fitting boots I've ever had. As the title says, "we'll see..."  As to quality -- these are really, really nice.  I've had several brands like Fry, Justin and Dan Post, and I really like what I'm wearing here.  I intend to update this review from time to time as these boots break in, so stay tuned.  Feel free to "reply" if you have questions.

Other reviewer's don't lie, the opening is so tight it takes some force to fit your foot in. On the flip side, they don't have that floppy feeling that other laceless boots or shoes have. Getting them off isn't as bad as I expected although there is no heal to lever against so I'm sure that the sole will begin peeling loose in short order, especially since it is glued on and not sewn.

Two big gripes with this boot: Unless you are Captain Hook, good luck getting a digit in the pull strap. I mean really, who checks this stuff at the factory? It's all I can do to get the tip of my finger in, I'm going to have to fab up a metal hook to aid in putting these on. The absence of a front strap is a major oversight.

Gripe number two is there is zero arch support in this shoe. Did they use retired NYPD flat foots to design this thing? They are decent to wear for a couple hours and there is so little room inside I don't think you could get away with adding an insert or arch support.

So to sum it up, for $50 it's a pretty decent boot that would work well for short stints of light wear. You will probably spend more time writhing on the floor trying to don and doff them than you will actually wearing the darn things. FYI I'm from the city of Eugene of which the boot is named so I am eminently qualified to review it I think!

Birko-Flor = Faux Leather. Not a leather upper. Don't get me wrong, this is a great sandal from a fantastic shoe maker BUT I had expected the "upper" - meaning the parts of the shoe that go over the foot - to be leather. It is not. The "upper" material is listed on the box as "Birko-Flor" which appears to be fancy, German-shoemaker-speak for faux leather. Feels like vinyl on the outer-facing side; feels like polyester felt or faux wool on the inside that comes into contact with your foot. Very comfortable, but am wondering how the faux leather is going to hold up over time. The footbed-liner - meaning the part of the foot your foot sits on - is actually leather. A final note about the color: "Graceful Toffee" is a glossy, metallic brown. It looks like glossy mid-value brown indoors, but outside, in the sunlight, it looks more metallic, like a warm bronze. Very pretty, but did I mention it's not leather??

I always do my research online. I usually love ASICS, and Amazon for that matter. I have to say, I have never been so disappointed by ASICS or Amazon.

I have a really serious pronation problem, which means that I need extra support in my shoes. I knew that ASICS have shoes that have great heel cups... little metal things inside the cushiony shoe that keep your feet from turning out of the shoe. After an ill-fated trip to the ASICS store where I was entirely ignored (I assume because I don't look like the typical runner), I did hours of research to find an ASICS shoe that had the "heel clutching system". I looked on the ASICS site as well as Amazon and several others.

My shoes showed up today, and to my surprise, the "heel clutching system" was no such thing. It was a mere plastic line that went around the heel and would have offered absolutely no support. The shoes were flimsy and seemed like they would break down with about one month of wear.

ASICS did not make this clear at all, and Amazon's absolute lack of information on these shoes was entirely misleading. So, I'm returning these and re-starting the hunt to see if I can find something decent. Beware the misleading information!

Huge upgrade from using Chuck's for squats and deadlifting.  I never believed in using a specialized shoe when I had a pair of perfectly good and flat Chuck's.  That is until a friend twisted my arm into using these shoes for a set of deadlifts.  After just one set, I was sold.  The wider toe area let my foot spread out flat and I actually felt a difference in my stability.  I will not try to convince anyone that a specialized shoe will increase their lifts but I will definitely vouch that you WILL feel more sturdy with 500 pounds on your back when squatting.  You WILL feel great when you get home and take your shoes off after a heavy lift day.  You will NOT feel that bunched up feeling in your toes that you may get with a pair of good ol' Chuck's.  The shoes are way lighter than they look and are pretty comfortable right out of the box.  I can only imagine how great these will feel after breaking them in for a year.  They got the extra padding near the ankles and the cupping of the heel right also.  I have no doubt that I will purchase another pair when these finally tear apart.

I really wanted to love these boots, but they weren't quite right for me. I have several other pairs of Ariat cowboy boots and they are generally true to size. These were the right size in width, but the top part of the boot where it's supposed to be snug across the instep barely touched my foot, and I have a very high instep. Normally I have to fight to get my foot into a boot because of my instep, even if they fit well once my feet are inside. But these didn't even have a "thud" sound when I put my foot in, like cowboy boots are supposed to - my foot just slid right in and out. If I kept them and let the leather break in a bit my foot would probably come straight out of them. I couldn't even go down a size because I'm a 5.5 and a 5 doesn't exist in this style. This is an issue that must be specific to the shape of this particular boot because it seemed to be the right size length- and width-wise and I've never had this happen with Ariats before. Very weird.

On top of that, the color wasn't what I expected. It's certainly pretty, but the first thing I noticed when I took them out of the box was that they were not as blue as I hoped (I purchased them in Waterfall) - I realize images on monitors can look different from real life, but every image I've seen of these boots on various websites was very clearly blue. When I pulled them out of the box, they immediately struck me as being much greener than expected. They are still blue, but they're a kind of teal that looks green when you hold it against something blue and blue when you hold it against something green. I really was hoping for true blue boots. Don't get me wrong, it's still a gorgeous color and very unique, just not quite what I was looking for. If the color had been the only issue, I probably would keep them. But the fact that they didn't fit well either means they're going back, despite the awesome sale price.

I think this is a great bag in general . The one I received does not have the mesh shown in the picture for the middle pocket but that one is an older model and the newer model that i received had a solid lining. This bag does not provide any secure type locking system or rfid pockets so maybe not ideal for travel. Easily fits more than you can imagine including a tablet but there is no padding ( might make it heavier). If you overload, although the wide strap helps, you might want to buy those wrap cushions designed for backpacks. Front strap pocket ok but too small for a phone or oversized sunglasses. Maybe could be used for a credit card wallet.

What I liked:
-Multi pockets
-lightweight
-water resistance
-attractive
-wide adjustable strap
-good size without being too small or large
-water bottle pocket ( although very tight squeeze for 16 oz bottle)
-side pocket for sunglasses

What could be improved:
-needs to have rfid pockets and maybe some toggle closure for more security.
-Zippers don't always glide easily so not sure about longevity
-Lining in middle pocket is taller than zipper so gets caught every time ( maybe I got a defective one)
- have at least one side zip pocket instead of all top entry ( slings twist around so you can easily access and most have a side entry pocket which makes it easy to leave the bag on your body while you access a pocket)
-would be nice if large pocket had a divider for more organization.
-Needs some updating for a quick access phone pocket that also fits larger phones (like the hidden quick grab ones that most of the travelon bags have)
-Wish strap could be used in either direction

The structure of this shoe is quite good. They are comfortable, well-supportive, and cushioned enough. Their greatest detraction is their aesthetic/upper design. The sole and heel are well-made, and the tongue is plush (unusual for Nikes these days), the upper, however is INCREDIBLY CHEAP. The ribbed/webbed structure beneath the thin upper mesh is cheap foam and the entire upper feels as though it would abrade from the slightest friction in the gym and/or after a few short runs. I do not recommend this shoe. Another example of profit-hungry Nike opting for cheaper materials under the guise of \\"breathable mesh upper.\\" Also, am I the only one annoyed by marketing bulls*** like \\"Lunarlon Ride,\\" \\"Fly-Wire,\\" and \\"Dynamic Support\\"? Call it what it is: cheap, run-of-the-mill foam, annoying little strands of twine, and a bunch of rubber and foam and s*** that cushions or supports you whilst you run. Nike marketing is ridiculous.

Update 9/29/15

So, after TWO MONTHS they are falling apart

1) the shoe is separating from the sole on both feet

2) the suede looking strip around the shoe is rubbing off

So, in a nutshell these shoes are falling apart after two months. They were comfy. I had high hopes, but two months of NYC walking and they are almost done for. These shoes are comfortable. No, you won't look like a fashion model in them. But, if comfort is your number one priority, these are a decently cute way to achieve that. I bought two pairs, the tan color and the grey.

1) The look- they remind me of my old skechers shape up Mary Janes that I loved but that skechers discontinued. And if you know that shoe, these are very similar. They are a bit of a big, clunky looking shoe. There is no way around it. That said, I do still think they are cute. The Mary Jane style makes them fun looking and cute. They can go with jeans, khaki short and a blouse, etc. They are the type of shoe you could wear with a workout outfit or with a summer day outfit of khakis and a blouse, or jeans and a cute top.

2) The fit- very true to size. They are not narrow, maybe run slightly wide. I am between a 9.5 and 10 and the 10 fit me perfectly. I ordered two pairs one in wide and one regular width. My foot leans toward the wider side but I usually just order medium width. Since these shoes are a bit wide I would have been fine with the medium width. Though the wide was not sloppy and I actually kept both pairs. The strap adjusts nicely for a secure fit. My foot was hugged nicely by the shoe and didn't slide around.

3) The comfort - very comfortable. I could wear it for hours right out of the box on the first wear with no blisters at the end of the day. That is saying a lot for a new shoe. I had knee surgery so this comfortable shoe is already a go to shoe. I walk in NYC for hours most days and this shoe has been perfect. It is supportive and comfy.

The positive: These shoes had a sturdy feel, but still had an amazing shock absorbing quality that protected my feet, ankles, knees and hips so well that I could jog farther without pain and there was significantly less post jogging aches. This mean a less painful and faster recovery, which got me back out running sooner, more often and for longer jogs. With most shoes I ran with, I always had some hip socket and ankle pain, no matter what techniques I tried.

The negatives: I think ASICS has a way to go on support, fit and the toe box area. While they didn't feel loose, they didn't feel like they offered much support in the way a pair of Salomon's due. Further, they should really start using a better lacing system that anchors back to the sole and uses the more modern pull securing system. Basically, I would love a pair of Salomon's with the sole of the Asics.

All that said

Be aware that the upper straps on many of the Arizona sandals are NOT real leather, they are called Birko-Flor, which is a synthetic with a fuzzy fabric backing.  Amazon does not specifically state Birko-Flor next to each color name. The description at the bottom of the page adds to the confusion because it is the same description for ALL the colors on their website, regardless of the strap material. While a few colors have the word "leather" next to the color name, and some others state Birko-Flor, the MAJORITY of colors say nothing at all. I assumed that if it didn't specifically state Birko-Flor in the color name, it was real leather. Not so! I ordered the Ice Pearl color and it is synthetic. You have no way of knowing this until you get it at home and it states so on the shoe box. Amazon should clearly state which colors are real leather uppers and which are Birko-Flor, so customers know what they will be getting. I am disappointed at Amazon for not being clear in their description,and at The Birkenstock company for compromising the sandal quality by producing them with an inferior synthetic material in the first place. If the rationale was to make them more comfortable by having a soft fabric backing next to the skin, this could still have been achieved with real leather on top and fabric on the back. I was eagerly awaiting these sandals, and now have to return them. At $110, I expect real leather, not "pleather".

The Boulder X was my second choice for an approach shoe. I initially bought 5.10 Guide Tennies, but the first pair was too big and when I went down a half size they were too small. So I jumped brands. I ordered both the Boulder X and the Mix (also La Sportiva). I was really impressed with the fit of both shoes (having European half sizes basically gives them American quarter sizes). The heel was snug and I had no slippage (something lacking in the 5.10s). The Boulder X has a lace around the heel that really snugs things up.

My overall impression of the Boulder X was that it was rugged. It has a nice looking edging zone at the front of the toe. The lugs were nobby and looked like they would be good for hiking. There is a lot of lacing, which is both good and bad since they are a pain to get on and off but there is a lot of customization to fitting. The rubber seemed pretty sticky, picking up plenty of dog hair as I tested them out around the house. They definitely look and feel more like a hiking shoe than a climbing shoe. They are a lot stiffer and have a lot more support than a climbing shoe or more minimalist approach shoe. Just testing them out in the house I noticed they were quite warm

While I liked the Boulder X I did end up sending them back in favor of the Mix. The Mix is much lighter and more flexible. The rubber seemed softer and stickier. The trade off is in support and durability. I feel like the Boulder would probably last longer and perform better on longer hikes. But currently most of our approaches are less than an hour, so I opted for the more comfortable of the two shoes. But if I find myself doing longer approaches the Boulder X is top on my list.

I have yet to put them to use in the field so I can't say 100% how much I like or dislike them, but have looked at all the major brands of gaiters as I have needed to replace a pair that is no longer made (which I loved) - and these are the ones I have decided I like the best. I use these for hunting elk in Colorado (requires protection from snow, mud, and moisture) so the fit has to be just right and I don't want sag as I walk otherwise the noise will kill me. Plus I am really picky about my gear. From wearing these around the house I believe they are the best option for what is out there today. OR Expedition Gaiters were significantly noiser, generally disappointed with the Black Diamond ones, and it was a split decision between these and the Kenetrek Gaiters (which are the only ones I can say are "designed for hunting"). I chose these over Kenetrek because 1) some guys has said the snow would "ball up" on the softer Kenetrek fabric and 2) the top clasp on the Kenetrek is not heavy duty and I could see it breaking in the field.
Fit is good on these but I did order a size down as I read other reports that they did run a bit big - I wear a size 10.5 mountaineering boot and I ordered a Medium for a good fit (MH Size chart said I should order a Large). Definitely durable/well made, all MH stuff is and if anything does break I know the company will 100% stand behind it. I read some reviews saying the top clasp was "tough to adjust", which it is but it is designed to be adjusted 1 time and then you don't touch it - and it doesn't move on you. For that reason I like it. Plus the little bit of elastic in the back helps a snug fit on the leg as well as to keep that top closure snug.
If they don't perform as I hope I will update this posting.

As advertised, great for keeping shape of shoes and letting leather breath. I have two Everlane Modern Loafers bought around the same time, wore equally as much, and in the same size 5. I can tell the difference between both with one using shoe trees and another without: the one with the shoe trees has a more supple leather feel to the interior, has better structure, and didn't need to be broken in. Excellent product, will definitely buy more!

Let me start off being clear as to what I do with work boots such as these. I work on a farm in Kansas and the conditions here are unpredictable and can be harsh. This is the main reason I went with this specific boot. I needed something that was durable, water-proof, comfortable and well built. Well, let me explain where the other two stars went. They are truly water-proof, no doubt about that. If that is a concern then rest easy, my feet stayed dry no matter how saturated these boots got! Comfort was a lil on the larger side of the 10.5D that i wear so I slapped some Dr. Scholl's work inserts in and they snugged right up. I would recommend a half size smaller or stop by a store and fit a few to be sure. The composite toe is a thing of beauty! It is light weight yet held up to cattle stepping on my toes and all while suffering no comfort loss. Now we get to the bad parts of the boot. I purchased these on April 6th and a little over a month ago the rubber sole on the tip of my right toe split horizontally. I figured no big deal, little glue and all is well, that worked for two days. Either way it split on a mold seam and is a hazard and a nuisance. Next the glue holding the leather to the sole has started releasing just behind my big toe on the right boot. the leather is peeling away form the rubber sole and is just a case of poor manufacturing. The same separation is happening on the left boot's outside arch. This is a smaller separation but the other started small and grew quickly. I don't abuse my boots and keep them clean and oiled often. So what I am getting at is it was the adhesive Justin Boots used in making these boots and not the leather that has failed. Very disappointed in this product, maybe I got a fluke, maybe they are all built this shoddy, but i don't feel they were worth $160.00+ and probably wont purchase another pair.

I have been wearing Belleville boots for the last 4 years. I was issued a pair of steel toes in the military and from then on was a Belleville fan. After separating from the military, I was hired on for a security officer position and had to find a pair of black boots. Naturally I turned to what I knew and I was excited to get a new pair of boots since I have been rocking the same 2 pairs of Bellevilles since 2011. Queue the issues.

The first thing I noticed was how difficult the boots were to get on. Specifically the right one. There is a hard notch that juts into the boot where the fabric meets the solid bottom portion of the heel. I don't know what you would call that but it was a major pain and is probably the issue that people complained about digging into their heel. Once I got it on I noticed that the "wide" fit was no longer the same "wide" that my old boots were. These were more narrow for my foot and were pushing in on my little toes. However the length was good. (I ordered same as my street shoe size). I thought these two issues would go away with a break in period so I tried them out.

Second issue was when I had to walk through the metal detectors at work. There appears to be traces of metal somewhere in the sole maybe that set them off. I'm not sure but now to get through the metal detectors I had to remove my boots and attempt to slip them back on on the other side. Now I thought, okay, I need another pair of boots but I can still keep these as a back up.

Third and final issue. After 1 week of wear, I went to slip my boot back on and the stitching on the back blew out. I'm assuming from the stress of my heel on the fabric was too much to handle and now I had a nice dime size hole.

That same day however I was contacted by the owner of outdoor sports asking for a review of the product. After responding and telling him my issues he put me in contact with the return department. I few emails laterthey sent me a pre-paid fedex label and said all I had to do was pack up the boots, slap the label on there and drop it in the nearest fedex drop box. I received my money back within a few days of dropping it off.

Final Conclusion (tl;dr): I had a very disappointing experience with the boots durability and fit and am sad to say that I have now turned back to Bates, but the customer service was phenomenal and I can't thank them enough for being so helpful.

I bought these when they came on sale, mostly on the strength of the extremely positive reviews. I wish I'd seen them in person first. One reviewer said they had great arch support. You can tell from the picture there is zero arch support. One review said they didn't flop when you walk, which sounded far too good to be true for a flip flop and most certainly was. These flop with the best of them. Several people said they could walk around in these all day with complete comfort, and I don't agree with that either.

Maybe if you're used to $2 Old Navy flip flops you'd like these. They'd be a step up from those, for sure, but overall these particular Skechers struck me as a cheap plastic shoe like the kind you buy at Payless (where I used to work.) One reviewer said she could "dress them up" by wearing something other than shorts, and I don't agree that they look dressy enough to wear to, say, dinner. They had a strong off-gas smell when I opened the box, and there is nothing to absorb sweat in the bottom. Great if you wear them to the pool, but if you're wearing them to walk around on a hot day (like summer) you'll be slipping and sliding if you sweat even the tiniest bit. Gross. Just a cheap shoe, out of many cheap summer shoes you could buy and probably already have a pair or two, like we all do. Just don't pay full price.

By the way, I normally wear a 7 to 7.5 in sandals, but I bought an 8 based on what some reviewers suggested and that was the right size for me so the reviews were right on the money there. Also, as many reviewers said, the straps thankfully aren't a hard plastic like you see in even cheaper sandals. They don't cut into your toes and feet. That's not enough for me to keep these, though.

I also really like that they come with a little bag for storing them in. I am always scratching my sunglasses when I throw them in my purse but I don't want a bulky case either, so the bag is a perfect solution.

What I didn't care for:
The only thing that I personally don't love about these sunglasses is the cat eye shape, it's just a little too "retro" for my taste and definitely makes them something that not all face shapes will be able to wear.

I have owned many, many Teva sandals - and have always been happy with them. A few years ago, I switched to a competitor and was also happy with the competitor's product. Last year, I decided to go back to Teva and purchase a pair of Terra-Fi 4. When they arrived, I loved them - they were comfortable and I could walk all day without any issues.

Then winter came and I put them in the closet, only to retrieve them this summer. Now, having worn them on and off for the past two months, I have severe damage on the soles of these sandals. This occurred despite the short period during which I could wear them and despite the fact that I no longer take long hikes (getting old, etc.). In essence, I got about seven months of wear out of these sandals.

After contacting Teva, I was given the cold shoulder and was informed about warranty terms which, however, is not helpful. In other words, Teva told me, in essence, that I cannot expect more than a few months of wear from these sandals. I find this unacceptable. Lesson learned - I will purchase a different brand in the future.

UPDATE: I contacted TEVA a few more times in hopes of getting an answer. Eventually, I was told that they evaluate problems on a case-by-case basis and to send my sandals in. I filled out a warranty claim form and stated, when prompted, that I would be okay with receiving the same sandal as a replacement.

A few days after my sandals arrived at their location, I received an email informing me that my purchase price would be refunded in total. While I certainly find a full refund an acceptable way of handling my complaint, I nonetheless feel rather sad. After all, this seems to indicate that the issues with my Tevas were not a mere manufacturing error but, rather, a representation of low quality soles that are being used in the manufacturing of this product. I will say that this is NOT something that I am used to with Teva - I have always known their products to be reliable and long-lasting. To me, this is a clear indicator that something isn't going right at Teva. I sincerely hope that such poor quality soles will be eliminated in future products so that Teva can remain a sandal with a great reputation.reliable and long-lasting. I was really excited to order the new Kayano 22. I had worn out my Kayano 21 from day to day use, gym use, and lots of running.

Looks: The seamless design will have to grow on me, it does not look as aggressive as the prior models.

Comfort: These have gotten too comfortable. They almost feel like grandpa walking shoes that you can wear all day long.

Feel: These shoes feel more flat footed than the previous Kayano's. There might be some mixed emotions on this, not as aggressive on the arch but in turn it seems that the flat design has brought in the comfort.

Running: I have done about 20 miles running so far on these shoes. Verdict... great!!

Size: I usually buy Kayano's about 1/2 size larger than my regular shoes. For me, the fit was great and did not appear much different from the Kayano 21.

Gym Use: squats and deadlifts seem more stable with the flat shoe design.

So far I am really enjoying me new shoes!!!

I've been running for years. Half marathons are what I enjoy. My favorite shoes for several years were Mizuno Nirvanas, which are unfortunately discontinued now. I've tried Mizuno Inspires, Asics Kayanos, and Brooks Adrenalines trying to find that next best shoe.

As a runner well into my 40s, I like the idea of something with more cushioning that the Hokas provide. However, I overpronate and a support shoe works best for me. Hoka never had that before the Constant.

I have a love-hate relationship with these shoes.

Things I love:
- The ride and cushioning is fantastic. My knees, hips, and back don't seem to get as beat up on longer runs when I'm wearing these.
- The low heel-toe drop makes my muscles work differently. I like it, but it does take a little getting used to.

What I don't love:
- Blisters. I get blisters on the outside of both big toes. Tried a 10.5 and it was too little. Went to an 11 and still get them, although not as bad. This has never been a problem in other shoes. Even my best socks and generous body glide on the offending area is not enough to stave off the blisters. The hot spots show up at 3-4 miles. Furthest I've dared run with these is 6 miles.

I wish they worked for me. I want them to work. I love the ride, but just can't deal with the blisters. My experiment with Hokas has been a failure, but your mileage may vary.

I ordered the wine-red purse for my mother to match her coat. I had the same experience as another reviewer, in that this was a bright red with purple lining.

It was also very stiff, and the zipper didn't work well. The gold zipper is very visible and really stands out; I would have preferred something a little softer with a less visible zipper, but that may be a personal preference. There's also a plasticky smell, but that would probably dissipate over time.

To be fair, there are several nice features too: The laser cutouts are nice and crisp (the side of each cutout is black when viewed at an angle), there are nice little feet on the underside to prevent scratching up the purse, and there is a small D-ring above the handle attachment on each side of the purse, for attaching a longer matching strap which is included. I ordered several items which arrived during a rainstorm, and this was the only one properly wrapped--there was a sheet of thin foam and the purse was wrapped in a plastic bag, so it didn't get ruined like the other items did.

Due to the color and the zipper issues, though, I am returning the purse. If the manufacturer plans to continue using the lot of red material used to make the purse I received, I'd suggest replacing the purse photo with a more accurate one, and revising the color description too--I see this more as a bright lipstick red.

I have worn LEMS shoes every day since their inception and have found the many pairs to be outstanding---however this current model represents an evolution of the product which is all too common among makers of "alternative" footwear. Earlier versions were of minimal construction, as close to barefoot as possible while still having a minimal covering--very supple and close fitting. What I have is more like a sneaker--it is more rigid and "built up". Not as clunky as a conventional sneaker , but clunky compared to Lems originals. The same thing happened to Tiger running shoes back in the 60's--they also added more support, thicker soles, etc. Does the public demand this??

I hope Lems returns to a more minimal shoe but I suspect that they won't and that they won't explain why.

I have a couple of pairs of Evo Wave Cursoris which I love. I do very short fast races and sprinting and have retired my first pair from running duty - they're still fine for slower runs or walking and are the most comfortable shoes I own. I've come to love the wider toe-box so my toes can splay out while running.

Wanting to try the closest thing in the Mizuno line to the Cursoris, and having read that the toe-box was wide in the Hitogami, I wrongly thought these would fit similarly to the Cursoris. Definitely pay attention to the shoe fitting application amazon has begun using to check fit before ordering. I ended up ordering these a half size too small. According to the shoe fitter the foot-bed exactly matches the size 12 asics I've owned. Because the shoe was too small for me to run in, I can only give superficial information on the shoe itself.

The materials and build quality is what I expect from Mizuno. The laces are a bit strange: they seem stiff and inflexible and appear designed not to slip between eyelets. I'm not sure how this would have worked out for me, as I rely on the slippage to fine tune lace adjustments while running. I either tighten or loosen the lace and let the change work down to the bottom of the lace while moving in the shoe. I can't tell how well these would do that, but the stiffness and surface roughness give me pause.
I returned them.

My only complaint about these high-priced sandals - The upper & lower foot straps are 'cheap-thin-leather' sewn to a synthetic backing that stretches-a-lot over a short period of time of use...and/or if the sandals become Wet. Well of course your sandals are going to get wet - that's what sandals are for, to wear around Water.
In my opinion, a $100 pair of sandals should have a durable and heavy quality foot strap to prevent stretching. The Vionics FAILED on this very important issue. Here is the damaging issue: The straps on these Vionics 'stretched-so-much' that the Velcro (which secures the upper strap to the lower) OVEREXTENDED it's reach to the lower Velcro-Strip so much that I could not fasten the Upper Strap around my foot. (there was no Velcro to fasten to on the bottom strap) So, I went to the local shoe-repair-man and had New and Longer Velcro-Strips sewn on the straps so I could fasten the upper strap to the lower.
Bottom-Line: These $100 Vionics are made with cheap-thin-stretchable-leather-straps, and are NO-Match for Quality of The Sperry Top-Siders at 1/2 the price of these Vionics.

Adidas Men's Superstar 3g Slide Sandals are, by comparison to other regular sandals, high quality materials, very well made (not excellent), and feels great to have on. Considering the pros and cons for this particular design, the pros are;
1. These are great pair of sandals to wear around the house, there are very comfortable at the bottom of the feet, as well as at the top strap.
2. The materials feels premium, I haven't seen or experienced any defect on these sandals except the logo and other prints on the feet pad rubbed off after 3 weeks of usage.
3. The design is beautiful, just by looking at it side by side with other sandals. I'm proud of wearing them around at the public pool, gym, and to the grocery store without feeling ashamed.
4. The cushion feels wonderful at THE BOTTOM of the feet when it is NOT wet.
5. Durable even after several washes in the washing machine.
The con
1. I can not emphasize on this enough!!! It is a terrible sandals to wear to take a shower!!! The foam absorbs water and it becomes unbearably annoying when walking around with soaked sandals around the house.
2. It will smell terrible, like a damped sock, if you wear them in the shower. The smell is hard to get rid of even if you wash them in the washer.
3. During hot weather, if you have sweaty feet, this sandals will be unwearable because the foam will generate heat around the top strap (yes, the top strap is padded). The padded strap is good for comfort when walking around but terrible if it is humid.

Overall, I'll give it 3 and half stars out of five. Despite Adidas the good intension of providing great comfort with the cushions and pads to make this sandals feels great, it is those same things that also set these sandals back, specifically when they are wet or when it is humid.

Having owned nearly all Xodus models (except for the original) and some even twice I have to say this is the least durable and most frustrating one of them all. These trail runners are designed to do big mileage at slower paces, thus the heavier weight and over designed outsoles treads vs other lighter/faster types of trail runners. Unfortunately this model does not hold up to the standard set by previous models when it comes to durability. The meager total mileage compared to the track record of previous models of the Xodus deserves the 2/5 star rating because a broken shoe needs replacement.

In all I would give the Xodus a 4.5/5 star rating as they are terrific for trail hiking and thru hiking. However I highly recommend skipping this model and going for the 4s or newer 6s

I have worn this model for several years. The first ones started to smell like cat urine (the outside of the soles near the heel) after a few months wear. New Balance replaced the first pair TWICE at no charge, saying a problem with some adhesive caused the shoes to develop an odor similar to cat urine. This year, they decided they would not replace them since they were way outside warranty. That is perfectly understandable. So I bought a new pair in January, 2015. Seven months later, one shoe started to have that cat urine-like smell again. It was only on the outside. So I contacted New Balance again. Here is their response:

"I am sorry you are experiencing an odor issue with your 990 shoes. I understand in the past we mentioned the odor issue is caused by an adhesive in the shoes. I would like to correct what we have told you in the past. After research and testing we have found the adhesive was not the issue in the odor issue. What we have found the odor is from moisture getting trapped in the shoe and the shoe not being able to completely dry out. The odor does take time to develop within the shoe if not completely dried out before the next wearing.

The 990 shoe does have a less breathable upper which makes it harder for the shoe to completely dry out before the next wearing vs. shoes that have a mesh upper are able to dry out at a faster rate than the 990 shoes. We have also found the best way to dry out the shoes is to remove the insoles, loosen the laces, and pull the tongue forward. This is the best way to dry out the shoes to help prevent the odor issue."

Thanks, New Balance, for the explanation. But please ponder this. One shoe of my current pair (7 months old) has that odd smell on the OUTSIDE of the sole, not the inside. If it is a moisture issue, why doesn't the inside smell like the outside? So far that question has not been explained to me by New Balance. And I don't believe I have exceptionally sweaty feet. Plus, this has not been an issue in any other shoe I own.

Again, these shoes are not cheap. And I will gladly pay for US-made shoes. And the fit and comfort are exceptional. But please consider the odor issue when purchasing.

This will be a slightly emotional review because once upon a time I really, really LOVED these shoes. I've probably had at least 10 pairs over the years. During winter months I wore them almost daily and in summer to work. They worked w/ jeans but equally worked with dress pants. Easy on and off in airport security lines was a major plus! Oh and they were comfy, oh so comfy. No break-in period needed. Best of all - they took a beating before they'd wear out to where even a shine didn't help. That's when I'd order a new pair. Best. Shoes. Ever!

I'd worn them around the world, London, Hong Kong, and then came the new pair and my trip to Japan ... and the failure. Now I had noticed the pair before this one wasn't up to quite the same quality. They didn't seem to last as long and like others who have reviewed this shoe the sole split in the end and I had to toss them. But I was in denial. They weren't quite as good but they were still my beloved Newports so I blindly ordered another pair.

The pair arrived promptly (thanks to Prime!) and I put them right on. They seemed even worse than the last pair. However, we've been together so long - I just wasn't ready to admit Newport had changed. Then in Japan I had to admit it - it was time for me to say goodbye to Newport. Yes, on my wonderful honeymoon, to the amazing land of Japan I had a full shoe blow out. While out seeing the sites it started to rain. No problem, I thought, we have rain coats and an umbrella. But the more we walked the more my feet felt soaked. Odd, I though, these shoes are pretty solid. I sat down and realized BOTH SOLES HAD CRACKED. Both. At. the. same. time! (photos provided) So cutting the day short we returned to our hotel room and I said my final goodbye to my Newports and into the trash they went. I sat, dried my feet, toasted what Newports once were and vowed to never be fooled by Eastland again - no more Newports!

My advice, don't get these. Eastland has decided to stop investing in the relationship. Find someone better, there's plenty of shoes in the sea.e. Eastland has decided to stop investing in the relationship. Find someone better, there's plenty of shoes in the sea.

I really, REALLY wanted to buy these sandals, but after trying them on at a brick-and-mortar store, I couldn't.  I typically wear a 9E (the circumference of each of my feet is about 10 inches, measured at the widest part just behind the toes), and the strap across the instep was a little short, although it mated with the Velcro patch acceptably.  The front strap, however, was MUCH too short; I'd have to add an inch of fabric to make it fit.  I'd like to believe that Dr Scholl was a fine podiatrist, but whoever has taken his licensed name has done it a terrible injustice.  For cryin' out loud, how much can an extra inch of Velcro cost?  The "hook" section is about 1/2 inch square, whereas the same corresponding section on my ancient Dunham sandals is easily three inches long and gives much more flexibility in fit and comfort.  The soles and non-adjustable back strap were comfortable and my toes didn't hang over the front as some reviewers have mentioned, but that front strap length was a deal-breaker.  If you have teeny tiny narrow feet, they might work for you.

Disclaimer: I have only had these shoes two days, but I absolutely love them. As others have said, there is no break-in. They came out of the box feeling like they were molded to my feet. I've worn them steadily preparing for a trip to Yellowstone and have had no areas of rub or chafe.
Sizing: the guy in the store told me to go up a half-size, and he was right. Both my husband and I felt our standard shoe size was too tight. I am normally a 7.5. The 8 felt just right, but I need orthotic arch supports. I went up another half-size to accommodate the arch supports and they fit perfectly. Usually I will find the arch supports raises the top of my foot too high and it rubs -- but not in these. Amazed there is no pressure there, no rubbing. My husband tried a half-size up. The length was good but width felt tight. Not all of the models offer Wide yet.
Based on the mid feeling so well broken-in out of the box I am doing a 2nd-day to get the same shoe in low for normal wear on the trip. I would never normally consider ordering a shoe 10 days ahead of a trip, but I am really confident these will surpass my current broken-in day shoes in spades!
One other comment. A big local independent outfitter has just begun carrying these. They told me a man came in and tried on every single hiking boot they have (~ 20 models) and walked all over the mall in each. He decided on the Oboz. They are very impressed and the kid who waited on me told me he couldn't wait until he could get a pair.

I love Clarks shoes. I know what size to buy, even on line, and they are always very comfortable (eventually). I say eventually since they all come a little tight for me in width, but within a few months they are perfect. And after 3 - 5 years, they are downright loose and have to be replaced. I especially like Clarks slip-on shoes since that's the kind of guy I am, but I always put these kind of shoes on with a long shoe horn. Yes, as one person mentioned, these shoes are a little tight on the top of the foot at first, but this is the way all Clarks slips-ons start. And since these particular shoes are waterproof, I would expect them to be a little stiffer than the others. After a few months, these shoes will fit like a glove, just like all of my other Clarks slip-ons. What sold me on this particular style was the looks and the bargain basement price. Maybe because this style is being phased out. However, in a few years, I suspect that I will regret not buying a second pair to replace these. I was originally after the nice looking Wave Quest GTX from Clarks, but at $160.00 a pair, I'll wait for them to come down in price, maybe next year. I'll do an update if I feel differently about these shoes in a month or two. As an aside, I was only able to get these from the vendor called "What's Hot" and they sent me a pair of shoes that didn't even come close to matching. Thanks to Amazon, the return and replacement was a breeze, but be carful when ordering from this vendor. They are probably OK, just an absent minded employee.
Update: 11/8/15 - These are now my most comfortable shoes, as expected after break-in. Yes, at first there was some tightness across the top of these shoes as a few have mentioned, but this is normal for well fitting slip-ons. The advantage of a slip-on is no laces to tie, but the disadvantage is that you have to use a shoehorn to get them on easily, especially if you want them to fit like a glove after break-in. Slip-ons that are too loose don't support you foot very well, especially when walking down hill. Love these shoes now. Might just buy another pair for when these wear out since the price is right.at are too loose don't support you foot very well, especially when walking down hill. Love these shoes now. Buying shoes online is one of the hardest things you can do which is why I hardly ever do it. I bought the exact same shoe that I had been wearing for the last couple of years but now it's almost a full size smaller! Shoemakers seem to think they can save on material and improve their profits if they make each shoe a little smaller. Let me explain why this is a really dumb idea. 1) People now have to buy the next larger size which will still use the larger amount of material and 2) People like me will just give up buying shoes online out of frustration. I just want a pair of shoes. I don't want repeated trips to the UPS store! I see lots of shoes online that I would like to buy but most of the time I think, "Well it's probably not going to fit" so I don't bother. Who wants to keep shipping shoes back and forth? I have better things to do with my time. Wise up shoemakers! Standardize the sizes and stick with that.

I bought these boots because of the reviews. Overall everyone seemed to really love them. A number of people even said that they were perfect from day one and didn't need any breaking in.

First of all, these are comfortable boots, with one exception. They have good support and form around your foot nicely. The only problem is, they mutilate your heels.

I took them for a three mile hike that I do a couple of times a week. After only 0.3 miles, my right heel was starting to hurt. After 0.5 miles my left heel started to hurt. By the end, they had torn the skin clean off both of my heels. I've never had any pair of shoes do that.

These things are built like tanks, without being overly large or heavy.

One thing I was not expecting was the grip. These boots have incredible grip. The only boots I've found with better grip are Innov-8 Rockilite. That's saying a lot.

Overall, these should be incredible boots once I break them in. I get the feeling it's going to take a long time to break them in. I'll try to do an update once they are no longer tearing the skin off my heels.

I'm a size 9 (occasionally a half-size down or up) over-pronator with a wide forefoot and an average heel, so I look for stability, motion control, and arch support in a running shoe in a wide size (D).  I've had good experiences with some men's Asics in a medium width, but I liked the looks of this women's shoe so much that I decided to try it.  I do love the looks, but alas, the D size isn't a true wide, which I can usually wear:  while the size-9 length of these fits me well, both the toe box and midsection are so narrow that the shoe is too snug even with thin socks.  They are extremely breathable because they're mesh, but because of that I'm not sure they'd stretch enough even with a professional stretcher to be wearable with crew socks, and they'd be quite uncomfortable to work out in.  Correction:  I got these in a size 9.5.  So even though I'm normally a size 9, that would be way too tight in this shoe.  Also, perhaps because they're all-mesh, they don't offer as much stability and cushioning as most of the Asics I've had, which is surprising, considering that on another website the manufacturers says they're "Built for mild to moderate overpronator who requires a blend of enhanced cushioning and stability."  I didn't notice either one.  I would have loved to be able to keep these shoes, but I'll have to return them and either go back to wearing men's sneakers or find something in a true wide.  PROS: Beautiful shoe, good price, looks great if you can wear it. CONS:  Not a true wide even if labeled "D"; less cushioning and compensation for pronation than some descriptions indicate.

I guess I am too naive about women's shoes. After spending hours in a shoe warehouse looking for ANY cross-trainers in wide widths and leaving with no options, I did some research online, then came to Amazon where I spent even more hours reading reviews. I purchased four pairs of cross-trainers and not a single one worked. Learn from my mistakes.

As a reference, my bigger foot (left) is 9.5" from big toe to heel, the ball is the widest part of my foot at just over 4" and my heel is 2.5" wide. I typically wear a size 7.5. I have a bunion on my left foot, very high arches, and my pinkie toes are a bit hammertoed from years in ill fitting high heels. I am making a concerted effort to wear better shoes, even if it costs me more. Unfortunately shoemakers refuse to recognize women with foot widths over 3". I ordered all these shoes in 7.5 D because I didn't see them any wider. That may have changed by the time you are reading this. Zappos tells me I should have ordered a size 8. Take my review for what it is: my one-time opinion.

Pair 1: NB 797 7.5 D. As I took these out of the box I knew they were doomed. Seriously, these don't look like "wide" shoes at all, I wish I'd gotten the "normal" B width to compare. This must be a joke, right? After putting them on I noticed what is a constant problem for me. The toe box of the shoe is too narrow, while the heel/opening is gargantuan. Pinkie toes are pinched and bunion is sensitive. Length is also cutting it close, like half a thumb left up front. Seems low on the sides but that's probably for the lateral movement. Also, the insole seems to be floating in the shoe. It's not too narrow, it's just not glued down so it seems like it might keep coming out every time I take the shoe on and off.

Pair 2: NB 711 7.5 D. These are the cutest in my opinion. I seriously want these to work. But they look so narrow. What. the. heck. Ah, super lightweight, not a lot of seams, narrower heel for sure. Also less padded around the shoe opening than #1. After leaving it on for just a few minutes, my bunion is throbbing slightly. That does not bode well. Length is cutting it close. If this came in an 8 4E, it would be a keeper.

Pair 3: NB 20 Minimus 7.5 D. These are definitely the least appealing to me. I mean they aren't bright white, but not my cup of tea. Anyway. Definitely the widest of the bunch. Ample room on the outside of the foot, it's weird, they sort of lean in, in a way that makes my bunion feel the bottom seam of the shoe. So it's not the pressure from the top or side that is irritating it, it's the lip of the shoe insert. Oh, there's no insert. Well I guess just where the upper and lower parts of the shoe meet. Looking at it you would be able to tell what I'm talking about; it definitely leans. The sides and back come up a little higher on the ankle, still not above the outer fibula bone-end, or the interior navicular bone-bump, so I like it more. I'm also clearly a dum-dum for ordering a 7.5. Still, this feels the best so far except for lean and it's effect on my bunion. Is the lean important to "minimal" shoes? Also, the box mentions running, which is not what I'm interested in. The listing online says cross-trainer.

Pair 4: Asics Gel Unifire 7.5 D. These are also a joke. The leather detail work on the uppers irritate my bunion from the start. The toe box is too narrow for the rest of my digits too. Actually the length on this seems better, but that is the only thing that is okay with this shoe. It feels nicely padded, I'll give it that. A big nope, all in all.

So all four of these go back, what a delight. This isn't a waste of my money and time at all, nooooo. I have now ordered NB Mens 623s and 608 in both 2E and 4E. I've also ordered from Zappos the Asics Gel 180 in 2E for women and the Asics Gel Acclaim in 2E for men. I think the biggest problem is Amazon's lack of selection (not even color-wise, just pure options!) at wide widths, and potentially no standardization of sizes, just like clothes. Are wide shoes just the unicorn of footwear or does Amazon just not care?

Although this product is accurately described with regard to the dimensions, they are very misleading. Depending on the construction of your eyeglasses they may not fully cover the lenses - they do not on mine. I carefully measured my lenses and found they were very close to the size of this product. However, the construction of my frames and the hing part of this product, caused them to sit very high on the frame, This caused almost a full 1/4 inch, at the bottom of my lenses, to remain uncovered by the product. In addition to a curved piece holding my glasses together, there is a top bar. This bar  running across the top of the frame, prevented the product from sitting low enough to cover that bottom 1/4 inch. My suggestion is to buy a pair of clip on sun glasses in a store, where you can experiment to make sure they will cover your lenses completely.

Update 2:  Actually the 14 4E were slight too big - I got some blisters because of them being loose. I 14 2E would be a better fit for the 13 $E. What interesting is that the shoe fitting tool says they'd both fit *perfectly*.   Buying shoes online is still hard. ---   Update: Amazon has introduced a new way to see if 1 pair of shoes compares against another pair.  So I entered that I had the MT910v1s and wore them in 13 4E and they fit perfectly and Amazon recommended (with a fancy 3d graphic) that the V2 size would be 14 4e.  Well I took a chance and ordered a pair and guess what -- they fit perfectly. Amazon gets a GOOD JOB from me.  New Balance gets a SHAME ON YOU for wildly mis-sizing the V2 or these shoes.  -------------  I was thrilled hearing that New Balance was making an updated version of the 910s, I still have my original pair (worn out but good for the beach) and would have re-purchased the v1s again if they were available.  The bad news about the v2's is that the toe box area seemed significantly smaller and my big toe and the toe next to it hit the edge of shoe while walking, making it a very uncomfortable shoe.  I experienced the same issue with there 1069s and it caused me to have x-rays done to determine if I had broken a toe  (I didn't.)  I returned them without trying a larger size because they stop offering .5 increments at 13 4E.  I hope new Balance starts sizing their shoes correctly. I'd expect a v1 and v2 to offer the same toe box space.  I gave them 2 starts because the black and orange coloring looks really good.

They were very comfortable once broken in, and I loved them with all my heart.
However, after all that, about 4 or 5 months into wearing these daily (mostly just around my college campus, no serious activity in them of course), they completely fell apart. Both shoes' buckles tore away from the rest of the shoe while I was just strolling down the street. I have honestly considered buying another pair because of how much I loved them at the time of wearing them, but they're fare too expensive to have broken in the first place, and I have no more faith in the brand.

I was extremely disappointed in this shoe, despite my loyalty with the Naturalizer brand. I've had these shoes just over three months and have worn them on nearly every weekend since their arrival. I wouldn't consider myself "hard" on shoes - when I've worn them, I've been outside on paved sidewalks and city streets.

First, the shoes have not worn well. The inside lining is continually coming out, and the seams between the sole and the red shoe leather has pulled apart at the toe area. The construction seems very poor.
Second, if the shoes gets even the tiniest bit wet (like when walking in a drizzle), the dye turns my feet totally red. It looks like I've waded through a bucket of red paint.
And third, despite trying nearly every weekend for three months, I can't seem to break the shoes in to my feet. I continually end up with massive blisters on my ankles and pinky toes every time I wear these. I wish I could return them for a refund, though they're likely too worn to do so at this point.

This was a poor investment, and not the Naturalizer standard of product that I have worn in the past.

UPDATED 7/15/15: I had originally rated these as 4 stars, but have had to change it to 2. I am wearing these shoes for the second time and am only a few hours into wearing them and am so disappointed that the loop that holds the strap down at the buckle just completely fell apart so now the strap is winging out with nothing to keep it down. Pretty disappointing to have them falling apart already even if it is only a small loop. :-( Not sure if I want to go through the hassle of sending them back or figure out a way to make them work, but a total pain in the rear either way and something that should not have happened after only wearing about 15 hours total.  ORIGINAL REVIEW: These shoes are great - so pretty and very comfortable! I feel like I have a pretty normal foot and the actual SHOE fits fine - what is a little on the large side though is the straps that go across the top of the foot. I'm wearing the buckle on the very last hole and feel like I will probably need to have my husband add another one as they stretch out. But you can see that the strap across the toes and over the bridge of the foot is not adjustable, so it fits somewhat loose on me. It doesn't make any difference in the wear - I did just fine in them, but I do need to stay aware so my ankle doesn't accidentally twist if my foot does slip because the straps don't fit tight.  On the other hand, the fact that the straps aren't tight meant that even at the end of a 10-hour workday and the regular swelling that you go through during a day, they still felt great at the end of the day and had no pinching or rubbing anywhere.  Overall, I love the shoes and will definitely get a lot of great wear from them! If you have wider or or fuller feet, you should be just fine ordering your normal size.
