I knew upon ordering that I would have to break these in due to the shape of my feet. Break in took about two weeks of wearing them around the house for a few hours each day.
I must use insoles for arch support cause my arches are so high. I have yet to find any type of footwear that can comfortably support my arches without insoles. So I knew with these boots, like any other, I would have some "growing into them" pains. For my feet, this is to be expected and I did not deduct any stars for that.
I deducted the stars for how hot these are on my feet. I purposely purchased these based on the reviews of others saying how well these kept their feet cool, even in desert climate.
My job requires my to walk off and on for 10 hours a day covering anywhere from 5 to 12 miles of open prairie, and the temperature can be anywhere from the 70's to the 100's.
I've worn FITS Light Hiker socks for years, in my opinion, they are the best for my feet. These Lowa boots are the first I've owned for my job that make my feet feel hot. They are consistent in their hot feeling too, no matter the outside temperature, my feet feel the same degree of hot.
I really enjoy the comfort of these boots, I feel as though I could walk for days and days without foot fatigue, I just wish they didn't feel so hot.

After wearing them on just one long relatively intense hike my feet felt terrible. This shoe made my feet extremely hot and the thin sole of the shoe made me feel as if I walked around barefoot all day. The thing that upset me the most is not the comfort level, but the sole of the shoe actually began to tear off of the side of the shoe after just one single hike. I had no choice but to return these after that, and I cannot say I regret it.

I've had great luck with these shoes, BUT I tried them on in store before ordering and found that I had to order about 1.5 sizes above my typical running shoe. These are a great trail shoe with a minimalist feel (light and flexible, low heel-toe drop, etc.) but maintains enough cushion for me to run ultra distance. I ran a mountainous 50k and had zero issues with them. The only issue I've had was with my first pair. I learned after a long run that I had to trim the sole insert due to rubbing under my arch. Likely, this was not an issue with the shoe itself, but an issue with how my arch sat on the arch. I would highly recommend to those looking for a lightweight, responsive, but sturdy feeling trail shoe. They do tend to split around the crease of the ball of your foot, others have complained about this, but it doesn't affect anything other than cosmetics. They're inexpensive compared to other ultra shoes, so worth it to me.

So i've worn these boots going on 2wks my work requires that i'm in and out of wet spaces quite often while working as boat electrician. Since my last post my BOGS are NO longer WATERPROOF I have thoroughly inspected my sole for puncture marks without finding any. I thought that maybe my foot (left) was sweating more than the right idk but it progressively got worse. I submerged the boot in a bucket of water and notice along the inside arch inseam were bubbles coming out when i applied pressure as if to walk. I don't know how long it has been defective because it wasn't until recently that i stayed in with boots fully submerged in water to notice. Since this discovery I am changing my review to 2 star. I will update when i hear from company on warranty or replacement, which really does me no good right now being that i'm in the Bearing Sea!

I've had these boots for a little over a week while working as an Electrician in Alaska on a fishing boat and they stand up to the beating i've put them through so far. I would say however that these are definitely worth the price and even with those 2 complaints i would purchase again. They are made tough especially if your in a wet construction site or marine vessel they're steel toe and have a thick sole that works pretty good. The review on these specific Skele toes is that they arrived in a crushed box packaged in a plastic mailing bag. One of the wraparound straps was far too large for the shoe and had, I suspect, come from another pair of larger shoes. That says more about the seller than the shoe. However, this style of shoe is much wider than the pair I just returned. The straps, even if they match the size shoe you purchased, are completely for aesthetics as the shoe itself is quite snug to begin with and there is nowhere near enough Velcro to actually snug the fit if you had to. Returned.

I had ordered a different style before this pair (Fila Skele-Toes Bay Runner 3 Lighted Sandal (Toddler/Little Kid/Big Kid),Monument/Castlerock/Hot Pink,12 M US Little Kid) and had to return them because they were faaaaaar too narrow for my daughter's foot. She had yet a different style last year (Fila Skele-Toes Bay Runner 3 Lighted Sandal (Toddler/Little Kid/Big Kid),Dazzling Blue/Lime Punch/Hot Pink,1 M US Little Kid) that were snug, as these types of shoes should be, and were a bit of a chore to get on. They did fit though. They lasted as long as it takes for a child to wear holes in them by dragging their feet to stop themselves on a swing...

All-in-all, I will continue to buy Skele Toes more because my daughter likes them than them being a good shoe for kids. You need to know that fit is going to run small and can vary greatly depending on the style of shoe you choose. It can be a wrestling match to get your child's foot into them.

I would suggest that buying them from a third-party seller, or even through Amazon, will run you the risk of receiving obviously worn returned items or complete knock-offs.

They will likely not last any longer than your average kid's shoe with regular use, especially due to hard tugging to get them on pulling at the seams and glue, and the thinness of the uppers of some of the styles.

It's a crap shoot...on pulling at the seams and glue, and the thinness of the uppers of some of the styles.

It is so uncomfortable! It didn't take me 5 minutes to decide this shoe was going back! Even at only $20.00, I couldn't see any reason to keep it. It hurt so bad trying to take steps in this shoe. I had a pair of the shoe pads for thong sandals and tried those. No deal. I boxed them back up and sent them back the next day. I love shoes and very seldom give one star reviews, but I wanted to make sure women buying this shoe realize that toe post is flat, plastic, and will probably cut into the flesh between your toes. I like specific info to help me decide, so I try to give that same kind of information. Hope it helps somebody.

I bought these shoes to use as "tennis" shoes (actually playing tennis) and they just won't work for that, at least for me.

The main problem is the back of the shoe is *very* low -- I don't wear high-tops or even mid range, but I need a bit of rise on the end so that as I move the shoe stays attached. These allow my heel to rise out the back even tightly laced, and so just won't work for any kind of strenuous movement (again, for me).

I ended up buying the NB 623 ( https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B007JTXJX0/ref=oh_aui_detailpage_o01_s00?ie=UTF8&psc=1 ) which DO have a rise at the back and fit me much better. They also are cheaper, a nice bonus.

Everyone's foot is different, and everyone's shoe's needs are, so these might work for you (and Amazon's return policy allows you to try them out). But overall I'd just pass on these and get the 623.

This is Susan's son, first off; the shoes were purchased for me, so I felt it made sense for me to write the review. And, as a bit of a forewarning, I'm in the process of sending these back as I type this review because these shoes just did not work for me at all.

I found these in a local shoe store and really liked them when I tried them on, particularly the lightweight feel, but they didn't have a size that fit well, so we bought them on Amazon a half-size up later that day. They arrived August 12 and I've worn them repeatedly in the three days since. However, after wearing them and being on my feet for a few hours at a time, I've come to the conclusion that these shoes would only work for a relatively small niche of people; that is, people with narrow feet who live in cooler climates.

Below the mesh upper is another, relatively thick layer of fabric that negates any breathability the mesh upper might confer to the foot; add to this the synthetic covering of the memory foam insole and you have a recipe for a shoe that retains heat very well. While this may be a blessing in colder weather, in Texas heat, it's a veritable nightmare for your feet as, after less than an hour, your feet will be hot and sweaty and you'll be wanting to remove the shoes with all haste.

Normally, I love Skechers' memory foam insole, as my Avillos have it and I wore those until the insole started coming apart at the seams and flaking off (I got a good several years' use out of them, so I'd say I got my money's worth; they're still functional even now), but with the way the insole is designed on these Persistents, if you have anything but a very narrow foot or tend to walk on the outer edge of your foot, the memory foam insole might as well be nonexistent. I took a measurement and, at the arch of the foot on a size 11, the insole measures a measly 2 inches in width; for comparison, my own foot at the same spot is 3.5 inches wide. Granted, I have a wide foot, so that's not the best comparison, but I've generally found with Skechers that I can reliably go up a half-size if a wide is not available (and it wasn't in these in the color we bought) and be perfectly comfortable. Not so this time. I found the inside half of my foot was resting off the edge of the insole, which was not the most comfortable experience.

Nevertheless, these shoes DO have some good points. I said this shoe was a good concept with a less than stellar execution and I meant it. The idea of a lightweight, flexible, and casual slip-on shoe that you could wear with most anything is a very attractive one and in those respects, it actually succeeds. Skechers states on their website that a Men's 9 weighs about 6 or 7.5oz (I can't remember exactly) and I wouldn't be surprised if the 11 weighed more than 8-9oz; they are very flexible and the upper has a good amount of give that I've only found surpassed by Converse All-Stars High Tops. And they are also casual, looking good with shorts or jeans or cargo pants, though whether or not they would actually go with anything is dependent on color (the black or charcoal, I think, would work with just about any outfit, the latter especially).

However, because of the shortcomings of the stifling upper and the too narrow insole, the places you actually want a shoe to succeed (particularly a walking shoe), these fall far short of what I was hoping for them to be.so that's not the best comparison, but I've generally found with Skechers that I can reliably go up a half-size if a wide is not available (and it wasn't in these in the color we bought) and be perfectly comfortable. I've owned several pairs of these now. I bought them.  I am not a major league umpire so I cannot buy a pair every few months. I do high school, college, and tournament baseball and women's fastpitch...easily 300 games per year. Have officiated for 40 years so have worn all brands of shoes for football, basketball, and on the diamond.  On a weekend I might do 9 - 11 games and even though only half of those are behind the plate they are simply made of poor material. The only part that will hold up is the flap.  As a shoe repair man told me: "These plastic shoes are basically a $39.00 cheap gym shoe with a flimsy insole, and then they add a plastic flap to it and charge another $80.00 for the flap. For occasional use these shoes will work. However for working 10 or more games per week you are up the creek because there are only a couple of choices.  Also, let it be noted that New Balance is not one of the better athletic product companies for acknowledging bad products and sloppy craftsmanship. Thus do not expect support from them at any level.  If I were 400 pounds I could understand these shoes lasting about 18 games. However I am 6' 0" and 195 pounds wearing size 13. They should hold up.  Keep in mind these shoes run narrow and a half size small. If they were $45 it would be a fair price. Remember there is no leather in these shoes so they will not breathe. If you're in cooler weather then it won't matter so much.

Here's some background on me: I have a wide forefoot and a narrow heel. I have a tendency to supinate in my natural gait. My use for these shoes was for general training indoors between weights, cardio, and infrequent classes. When I realized my old shoes (also New Balance) were no longer cutting it, I contacted New Balance support for suggestions, and this was a recommendation based on my needs. I purchased these in my usual 8's in a wide - same as my last pair of New Balance shoes. These shoes offer cushion, and the foam has great responsiveness. At first, it feels like walking on air. Going from a studier shoe to the 822v2 was a completely different experience -- one I very much enjoyed.  After a few sets of lunges and squats, I noticed the fatal flaw in these shoes when my feet were inexplicably unsteady. With my narrow heel, this shoe offers no support and stability at the back of the shoe. The heel is basically all foam padding without any structure. This caused a lot of slippage. After switching to cardio, I noticed the heel bunching up at the bottom where the heel meets the sole of the shoe. I tried lacing them tighter and with different lacing patterns to keep my heel in place, which only caused my foot to go numb towards the outside metatarsals.  I discussed this with my chiropractor, who is also a fitness junkie. She was once brand loyal to New Balance (as I am/was), until a fellow student in school did a study wherein they deconstructed multiple brands of athletic shoes. New Balance rated really low on construction and actual foot support. She mentioned Saucony, Brooks, and Asics for great gym shoes (I've decided to try a pair of Brooks).  So while these 822v2 shoes look great, weigh almost nothing, and make you feel light as a feather, they don't quite offer the necessary support when needed. They do run true to size where length is concerned, though seem to run a little narrower. I will still keep these shoes for running errands around town, but they don't fit the purpose for which they were purchased.  UPDATE: I'm coming back a short time later to say these shoes are truly a disappointment. I used to love the New Balance brand, but these are a joke. You'd be better off purchasing a piece of foam and affixing a lace up sock to the top of it. These provide absolutely no structure to stabilize your foot. Waste of money.

Shagwear designs some of the cutest wallets out there. Prefer an eye-catching design over a $90 metal name-brand label on a tired print? Then these may be for you.

Over the past five years, I have owned three Shagwear wallets (their Canadian flag/maple leaf, the travel passport style, and a floral one). They are conversation starters wherever I go. People really want to know the story behind a stylized Canada goose or whether that's the Canadian flag on the back of my wallet. Yes, yes it is. Am I Canadian? Yes, I am. No, I don't know your cousin Bob in Toronto but let's talk about health care now.

Shagwear would earn more points if they made their wallets to last more than a year. I'm not really that hard. I do not whip out wads of cash or pile in loonies and toonies by the dozen. Nor do I carry 72 loyalty cards because I don't think I can name 72 Canadian stores, let alone shop in that many. Unfortunately the seams on their wallets were sewn by gophers in Albertan mountain camps, pressed down by little paws, and no actual use of string or thread appears to meld the sides. The seams split easily when aggressively used -- say, by slipping your debit card into a slot once a day. It tears or wears away at the worst spots, right where cards line up or along the middle seam separating the cash pocket. Argh.

Be gentle with zippers that tender hands have sewn in because they aren't made for a lot of wear and tear. Or rip and zip. They tend to pull away at one end and there's no way to really secure the zipper once it comes away.

I've worn and used Aasics for over a decade now. But I thought I'd try out a different brand, especially due to different reviews I'd read praising these Ride 7's.

It's a decent enough shoe...until the sole seemed to fracture itself in my right shoe in the forefoot. It's been hot so I haven't been running for about a week and a half, just walking. It broke into about the month mark, but probably it would have been by the 3 week mark otherwise.

I rarely return things, and especially for a running shoe all I'm ever really looking for is a year at most, depending on mileage too. Aasics has generally been good even longer than a year, and runs the same at mile 500 that it does at mile 10. I barely got maybe 30 miles on these things before they were no good.

Maybe you won't get a bad pair. I just sent them for a return, and hopefully I get a full refund (I did buy a box to ship them off in, but I don't stress over that minimal amount). But I'm just putting this in here in case others have the same problem, in which case the reviews will add up and people should avoid it in that case. If I'm an isolated incident that's fine, maybe they have a small failure rate and things like that happen. If I'm not, then it will show in the reviews.

Four years ago, I purchased this Merrell shoe from a local retailer and was pleased with the fit and durability. This old pair is starting to wear, but the sole remains intact and attached to upper shoe body. With this good experience, I decided to buy a second pair online through Amazon. Price was about the same as local retailer, or maybe even slightly higher. The shoes arrived on schedule, new, and fit as expected.

However, although this pair is 6 months old, the sole is separating from the shoe body. The gap is about 2 inches long and looks as if insufficient glue (or poor quality glue) was used to attach sole to upper shoe body. I've had to inject glue into the gap to keep sole from further separation.
I like the Merrell brand, but now wondering about their manufacturing quality control. Maybe these shoes are 'seconds' (but not marked or advertised as such). Maybe these are not Merrell's, but instead a knock off copy. Don't know.
I do know I'm NOT happy and do not plan or expect to order any more shoes from Amazon.

I've owned a couple pair of the Classic Crocs and found them amongst the most comfortable shoes I've ever owned - while the tread is unworn. As it wears, it becomes more slippery when wet. I bought the Bistro Clog because its sole solves the possible hazard. However, this pair is the first non-vented Croc I've gotten. I found, when wearing the shoe indoors exclusively,  that I had water build up from body heat. I needed to dry the shoes twice during the time I wore them. I was wearing socks for medical reasons, and the wicking of the fabric became an issue. I'll never get an unvented Croc again. However, the main reason I write is about the fact that the two shoes were NOT the same size. The opening to the left foot was a full 1/4 inch larger than the right foot. Sitting side by side they looked like two different sizes - which of course they weren't as they are molded. It is the opening that is a quarter inch different - which in my case makes the foot slide too much. I assume each foot is molded from a different mold. I'm not clear how that great a variation can happen, but it is enough to make the left "too large" for me as it produces too sloppy a fit. Crocs are made in different places. These say "made in Vietnam". The Classics I'm wearing as I write this were made in Mexico; they're fine and fit well, but they have the traditional sole that becomes very slippery in time - not so safe for these old bones.

I have bought and worn 4 pair of these MW411 walking shoes.  I love the fit and feel.  For fitness, I used to run and wear running shoes but now, as I'm getting older (late 50s), I try to walk as much as I can every day.  My goal is to walk 5 miles a day and I'd say I come pretty close to that.  So my point is I walk a lot in these shoes.  My concern is I don't get full use out of these shoes due to poor bonding of the glue between the sole and shoe body.  Every single pair has failed in the exact same way.  The bottom separates from the body at/near the toe area then the separation gradually gets worse until the whole sole is separated down to the middle of my foot.  At that point, I throw them away and buy another pair.  I haven't tried gluing the sole back to the body as I've seen in other reviews.  The shoes fail at about the 4-5 month point for me.  Given my walking habits, that's perhaps 500 to 600 miles walked.  So I can't fault the shoe entirely.  That's a lot of miles!  But I do think New Balance should recognize this defect and use better glue.  I'd like to wear a hole in a pair or have the sole wear thin just once instead of watching what looks like a perfectly good pair of shoes come unglued!  My plan is to try another brand of walking shoe when the pair I have fails ... the soles are already starting to separate ... again!
